coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic magnetic properties of plant leaf-derived graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown copyright © 2016 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chuang, Chiashain
Roy, Prathik
Ravindranath, Rini
Periasamy, Arun Prakash
Chang, Huan-Tsung
Liang, Chi-Te
coredata.dc:description: Hexagonal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from plant leaves with diameters in the range of 5–7nm with zig-zag edges were synthesized and its intrinsic defects and magnetic properties were investigated. Our results showed paramagnetic behavior below 15K. The origin of paramagnetism was ascribed to the edge spin effect presented in the GQDs and largely based on the GQDs hexagonal nanoislands with zigzag edges. Our results are further consistent with the theoretical prediction. The excellent spin and magnetic properties exhibited by these GQDs could potentially lead to new and advanced nano spin-manipulation devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of GQDs, inset to 1A shows the 2D FFT image of the crystalline hexagonal structure of these GQDs (B) the full XPS spectrum and (C) deconvoluted C1s spectra of GQDs.

(A) Magnetic response and fitting of the GQDs. Fits of the data at T=2K to the Brillouin function with various values of J. (Inset: magnified fit of T=2K to the Brillouin function vs J =5/2). (B) Susceptibility versus inverse T plot revealing a linear dependence and purely paramagnetic behavior without sign of magnetic ordering.
Fits of the data at different temperature using the Brillouin function with J=5/2.
The modeling number of spins per gram with different diameter of the GQDs.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic magnetic properties of plant leaf-derived graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Taiwan University, Taipei 106, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, National Taiwan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 106, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Program, Taiwan International Graduate Program, Academia Sinica, Taipei 11529, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience and Technology Program, Taiwan International Graduate Program, Academia Sinica

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexagonal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from plant leaves with diameters in the range of 5–7nm with zig-zag edges were synthesized and its intrinsic defects and magnetic properties were investigated. Our results showed paramagnetic behavior below 15K. The origin of paramagnetism was ascribed to the edge spin effect presented in the GQDs and largely based on the GQDs hexagonal nanoislands with zigzag edges. Our results are further consistent with the theoretical prediction. The excellent spin and magnetic properties exhibited by these GQDs could potentially lead to new and advanced nano spin-manipulation devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Derivatives of graphene such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoribbons (GNR) exhibit substantial quantum confinement and edge effects that allow GNRs (<10nm) to have semiconducting properties. GQDs while possessing strong quantum confinement and edge effects (<100nm) [1–4] show interesting magnetic properties similar to GNRs when their sizes are reduced below 10nm. However, limited attention has been paid on its spin and magnetic properties owing to its edge effects. The strong quantum confinement edges of GQDs reveal excellent wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, in contrast to zero-band gap two-dimensional graphene sheets [5–7]. GQDs with sizes of around 20nm have been synthesized via lithography techniques [8] though this is expensive and does not always lead to the formation of smooth edges. Chemical routes such as reduction techniques have resulted in sizes from 10 to 50nm. Additionally, not many techniques produce consistently smaller sized GQDs (<10nm) [9–11].

We have previously prepared natural and inexpensive plant leaf-derived GQDs, from a sustainable and renewable source of Neem (Azadirachta Indica) leaves through our hydrothermal techniques [11,12]. The excellent photonic properties exhibited by GQDs has meant that it has been developed into solar cells and nanolasers [13], which are of great use to the modern energy hungry society. On the other hand, spin and magnetic properties of GQDs is an area that has not received as much attention despite the spin magnetic moments from the intrinsic GQDs defected lattices being important issues to study in order to develop polarized spin-nanolasers [14] and solid-state quantum networks necessary for manipulating the polarization of a photon [15].

In this report, we have synthesized GQDs from plant leaves and investigated the magnetic properties that were further confirmed by the lattice structures, sizes, defect conditions and the compositions of elements. The features and intrinsic magnetic properties of the leaf-derived GQDs have been recognized systematically, and the origin of magnetism has also been discussed.

Fig. 1A shows the high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of the GQDs exhibiting mono-dispersed and hexagonal shaped GQDs with an average size of 5±0.27nm (50 counts). Inset to Fig. 1A shows the 2D fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of the crystalline hexagonal structure of these quantum dots. The GQD edges mostly follow the zig-zag orientation, although other orientations are also possible [16,17]. A greater majority of all GQDs in Fig. 1A preferred the zig-zag orientation which also give us an insight into its formation mechanism. The GQDs were formed by breaking of the graphene oxide sheets along the zig-zag direction and extracting sp
2 domains from oxidized graphene sheet via a bond scission of surrounding oxygen groups. Previous studies have suggested that these free zigzag sites are mostly carbene-like [18]. The photoluminescence properties of GQDs are also said to originate from these free zigzag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state, which is natural coming from the breakage of the graphene oxide sheets [17]. As seen in Fig. 1B, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed a dominant graphitic C1s peak at 284.4eV and O1s peak at ca. 530eV for GQDs. The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1C) confirms the presence of C=C (284.4eV), C–O (285.6eV), C=O (285.8eV) and COOH (288eV) bonds indicating the GQDs were functionalized with carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups [10,17].

Fig. 2A shows the magnetization of the GQDs by superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) measurement. Dried and pure GQDs (4.49mg) were carefully collected and sealed into a plastic capsule in order to avoid any contamination and measured by (SQUID, Quantum Design MPMS) at various temperature from T=2 to 300K. The behavior is well fitted by the standard Brillouin function:

=
NgJ
μ
B
{
2
J
+ 1
2
J
c o t h
[
(
2
J
+ 1
)
x
2
J
]
−
1
2
J
c o t h
(
x
2
J
)
} ,
where x=gJ
μ
B
H/
k
B
T, g the g-factor, J the angular momentum number, N the number of spins and
k
B
the Boltzmann constant. Assuming g=2 as usual carbon material electron spin resonance measurements [18,19], the standard Brillouin function provides a perfect fit only for J=5/2 as shown in the inset of Fig. 2A. For J=5/2, the number of spins per gram is N=4.08
×
1018/g. Such a result is consistent with the Curie law M(T)/H=[NJ(J+1)g
2
μ
B
2
]/(3k
B
T) with J=5/2 and N=4.31
×
1018/g with external field H=1kOe as shown in Fig. 2B. As shown in Fig. 2B, the good linear fit of the slop means the paramagnetic behavior with the Curie constant ([NJ(J+1)g
2
μ
B
2
]/3k
B ) and the spin follows the Curie law (M=CH/T).

In order to further confirm the temperature dependence of magnetization in the GQDs by the standard Brillouin function, we measured the magnetization curves which were fitted well at different temperatures as shown in Fig. 3. For T=5, 10 and 15K, the number of spins per gram are 3.57
×
1018/g, 3.14
×
1018/g and 2.69
×
1018/g, respectively. For T=100K, the magnetic properties changed from paramagnetic behavior to diamagnetic behavior, which indicated that the thermal energy k
B
T gradually suppressed the spin effect of the GQDs. This diamagnetic behavior of the temperature-independent intrinsic carbon material results due to the Lentz law from carbon atomic electron orbital magnetic moments so as to reveal negative magnetic susceptibility when the positive magnetic susceptibility from paramagnetic electron spin moments decreases to zero by increasing temperature. Such interesting magnetic transition and electronic transport results were also reported from graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide, since the GQDs are obtained by breaking down graphene oxide and could form quantum dot array in a two-dimensional plane [20–22]. According to our results, we suspect that the observed paramagnetic property in the GQDs is due to a single formation with μ=gJμ B
≈
5μ B with concentration
≈
80ppm, which is around one moment per 12,500 carbon atoms. For the 12,500 carbon atoms, we considered the graphene area density n
≈
0.76mg/m2 and our GQD diameter d=5–7nm with few layers. We approximately estimated that each of the GQD existed in one moment μ
≈
5μ B. Based on our characterization results from HRTEM, XPS and SQUID data, we eliminated the intrinsic paramagnetism with J=1/2 due to individual electron magnetic moment from vacancy or adatom that produced localized moment so as to totally contribute to J=5/2 for one GQD [23,24]. We suspect that the magnetic moments in the GQDs arise from the zigzag edges. According to Fernández-Rossier and Palacios's theoretical predictions, the nanometer-sized graphene structures with hexagonal shape above a critical size of 1.5nm develop local moments from the zigzag edges [2], highly correspond to our GQDs with zigzag edges, hexagonal shape and the same order magnitude of the GQDs size. Most importantly, the GQD with hexagonal shape existed in zigzag edges have been proven in both theory and experiments [26,27]. Furthermore, our results are highly consistent with results obtained from graphene nanocrystals produced by sonic exfoliation of graphite with typical sizes of 10 to 50nm, which is the same order magnitude of the number of spins per gram N~1018/g and with J=5/2 [19]. These nanometer-sized graphene structures islands in magnetism further support that GQDs with zigzag edges could exhibit magnetic properties [25, 28,29].

It is worth exploring whether the magnetization signal from our GQDs decrease by increasing the quantum dots size due to the different edge to surface ratio. According to above results for J=5/2, our GQDs wth d=5–7nm of few layers reveal μ
≈
5μ B for each GQDs in N~1018 spins/g. Therefore we know N
∝
1/d 2 for the same weight of GQDs with different d. Based on our results, Fig. 4 approximately shows the relation between the diameter (d) of GQDs and the number of spins per gram (N). Consequently, N decreases with increasing diameter of GQDs due to the reduction for the number of GQDs as shown in the figure. In comparison with Fig. 2A and the standard Brillouin function, the magnetization M is proportional to the number of spins per gram N for J=5/2, T=2K and g=2. Therefore, the background magnetization signal of the commercial SQUID system at T=2K is around 10−6 Am2/g, which is around N~1016 spins/g in our GQDs. For those with larger diameter than the critical diameter (d c)~158nm of our GQDs, the magnetic signal cannot be measured by SQUID as the magnitude of magnetization is smaller than the SQUID background magnetization as shown in Fig. 4. Hence, our cost-effective and green GQDs (d<10nm) possesses great advantage for revealing strong magnetization due to its edge effects that are based on the quantum confinement effect with its nano-scale size of GQDs.

Consequently, the spin properties combined with the photonic properties due to the quantum confined condition in the GQDs could reveal single-charge, spin-manipulation and light-manipulation characteristics and pave the way towards novel nano-devices developed from natural sources such as plants.

In summary we have studied the spin and magnetic properties of GQDs with size (<10nm) synthesized from natural neem plant leaves. Our studies revealed that the GQDs exhibit paramagnetic properties at low temperatures and diamagnetic property above 100K, which suggests the spin effect is due to the edge defects in the GQDs. These exciting revelations could lead to a better understanding of the physical characteristics of the newest member of the graphene family and the development of path-breaking new nano spin-manipulation devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Science Council (NSC), Taiwan (grant no: NSC 101–2113-M-002-002-MY3). P.R., C. C. and C.T.L. acknowledge support from the NSC and MOST (grant no: NSC 101–2923-M-002–003-MY3 and 102–2119-M-002–016-MY3). P.R., C.C. and A.P.P. are grateful to the MOST and National Taiwan University for a postdoctoral fellowship under the contract number NSC 104–2811-M-002–154, MOST 104–2811-M-002–116 and NSC 104–2811-M-002–154, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.01.130.
